topic: A teacher receives an unexpected gift from a former student
raw_output:
[story 1]

Professor Eleanor Vance smoothed the lapel of her tweed jacket for the tenth time, a nervous habit she hadn't shaken since receiving the annual faculty newsletter. The small, intricately carved wooden bird resting on her desk felt like an accusation. It was a gift, clearly from Marcus, a student she hadn't seen in five years, a ghost from her undergraduate days. Her fingers traced the smooth, cool wood, the movement jerky. She glanced toward the door, half-expecting him to materialize, ready to offer a clumsy apology or perhaps a poorly phrased compliment. The silence in her office was thick, amplifying the frantic drumming in her chest. She picked up a pen, its plastic casing slick beneath her grip, and found herself staring instead at the tiny, perfect wings of the carving, wishing the floor would simply swallow her whole.

[story 2]

"Oh, my goodness," Eleanor murmured, her voice sounding far too high as she examined the velvet box. The gift—a vintage fountain pen, exquisitely engraved—was heavy in her palm. She kept turning it over, the gleam of the metal reflecting the harsh overhead light, making her feel exposed. She avoided making eye contact with the door, acutely aware of the way her collar felt too tight against her neck. She rehearsed a dozen polite, detached responses in her head, all of them sounding brittle and inadequate. When she finally did speak, the sound was a little shaky. "Marcus," she managed, the name tasting foreign on her tongue. She felt a distinct tightening in her throat, a desperate urge to retreat behind the heavy oak of her desk, to become invisible.

[story 3]

The unexpected package sat on the corner of Eleanor’s cluttered mahogany desk, a splash of bright, unfamiliar color against the muted tones of her academic life. Inside, nestled in tissue paper, was a collection of rare botanical prints. Eleanor picked them up with both hands, the paper crinkling under her touch. Her breath hitched. She felt a flush creep up her neck, warm and undeniable. She kept glancing toward the hallway, imagining Marcus standing there, perhaps looking awkward, perhaps hoping for some acknowledgment. She wanted to shove the prints into a drawer, bury the evidence, but her hands remained frozen, unable to move away from the object that felt so intensely personal, so jarringly out of place in her carefully curated professional space.
